topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a lead weight. I clicked it open, my thumb hovering over the screen, expecting the familiar warmth of laughter and balloons. Instead, the stark, impersonal text confirmed it: "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise celebration for [Name] has been cancelled." A slow, cold dread began to coil in my stomach. I reread the message three times, the words blurring slightly. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room, the hardwood floor protesting under my restless steps. My jaw tightened, and I ran a hand through my already messy hair. Who would have done this? The anticipation that had been building for weeks suddenly collapsed into a heavy, suffocating silence. I let out a short, sharp exhale, the sound brittle in the quiet apartment.

[story 2]

I stared at my phone, the notification blinking mockingly. Another cancellation. The air in the room seemed to thicken, pressing down on my chest until it was hard to draw a full breath. I started tapping my fingers rapidly against the desk, a nervous, uneven rhythm. Every surface in the room seemed to mock my hope—the half-inflated banner I’d bought, the carefully chosen playlist, the excited glances I’d exchanged with the planning committee. A low growl escaped my throat, a sound born of sheer disbelief. I leaned back in my chair, my shoulders slumping as if the weight of the disappointment had physically settled upon me. I picked up a pen and began aggressively scribbling meaningless lines on a notepad, the ink bleeding slightly where my grip was too tight.

[story 3]

When the notification finally registered, a strange hollowness washed over me. I felt a sudden, intense heat rising in my neck. I stood by the window, looking out at the mundane street below, unable to focus on anything else. My hands clenched into tight fists at my sides, the knuckles turning white. I let out a sound that was more of a sharp, wounded sigh than an actual word. I turned away from the window, walking to the kitchen and slamming the cupboard door shut with unnecessary force. I leaned against the counter, breathing in short, shallow gasps, my gaze fixed on the empty space where the party was supposed to be. The energy that had been buzzing with excitement evaporated, leaving behind only a heavy, inert residue.
